title: Decoherence of spatial superpositions along stationary worldlines
authors: Clemens Jakubec, Aaron Bartleson, Peter W. Milonni, Kanu Sinha
abstract: We analyze the decoherence of a particle's spatial superposition moving along a stationary worldline through the Minkowski vacuum. The particle is modeled via an internal degree of freedom that couples to a scalar field, and an external degree of freedom, i.e., its quantized center-of-mass motion around the stationary worldline. Assuming a separation of time scales between the particle's internal and external dynamics, we first obtain an effective red-shifted polarizability of the particle, characterizing the trajectory-dependent linear response of the internal oscillator to the field. We then derive a quantum Brownian motion master equation for the particle's center of mass, under the Born-Markov approximation, which describes its decoherence in the position basis, as well as, Hamiltonian modifications corresponding to a dispersive potential. The resulting decoherence has two components: (1) arising from a modified field spectrum observed by the particle; and (2) due to a differential time-dilation over the particle's extended spatial wavefunction. For stationary trajectories, both contributions take an effectively thermal form. We evaluate the decoherence rates for two specific cases of hyperbolic and uniform circular motion.
summary: This paper analyzes how a particle's spatial superposition decays due to relativistic effects while moving along non-inertial paths. It distinguishes between decoherence caused by the thermal-like Unruh effect and that caused by gravitational time dilation. The findings show that both effects contribute to the loss of quantum coherence and the emergence of new dispersive forces.
main_problem: The paper investigates the mechanisms of decoherence and the emergence of dispersive potentials for the center-of-mass motion of a particle moving along stationary, non-inertial worldlines in the Minkowski vacuum.
main_result: The authors identify two distinct decoherence components: one arising from the Davies-Unruh effect (field spectrum modification) and another from differential time dilation across the particle's spatial wavefunction.
method: The study employs a quantum Brownian motion master equation derived under the Born-Markov approximation, utilizing spectral decomposition of field operators and a separation of time scales between internal and external dynamics.
model_or_system: A polarizable particle with an internal oscillator (internal degree of freedom) and a quantized center-of-mass motion (external degree of freedom) interacting with a 3+1 dimensional scalar field.
key_observables: Decoherence rates (Davies-Unruh and time-dilation components), momentum diffusion, dissipation, and dispersion potentials.
important_parameters: Local acceleration, redshift factor, Davies-Unruh temperature, and the ultra-relativistic limit for circular motion.
main_assumptions: Stationary worldlines, separation of time scales between internal and external dynamics, Born-Markov approximation, and the particle's center-of-mass wavefunction is sufficiently localized.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares decoherence mechanisms in different spacetimes; Figure 2 provides a schematic of the particle model; Figure 3 illustrates the two physical perspectives of decoherence (inertial vs. co-moving).
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the particle model and interaction, proceeds to derive an effective red-shifted polarizability, constructs a quantum Brownian motion master equation, and concludes by evaluating specific cases of hyperbolic and circular motion.
why_interesting: It provides a fundamental link between relativistic effects (Unruh effect and time dilation) and open quantum system dynamics, specifically how spacetime geometry induces decoherence and forces in a quantum particle.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Summary of commonly studied decoherence mechanisms in flat spacetime as well as curved spacetime. (a): Unruh- DeWitt field detectors with a classical center of mass consisting of either a two-level system (with frequency ωi) or a harmonic oscillator have been studied in a plethora of situations. Along a hyperbolic worldline such a detector will see an effective temperature TDU. The thermalization and decoherence of such detectors has been studied in [10–18]. (b): Our model (see Fig. 2) where a particle with an internal oscillator degree of freedom in a spatial superposition of ˆ Xi(τ) is coupled to a field via its polarizability combines the decoherence mechanisms of (a) and (c)....

FIG. 2. Schematic representation of a particle with center- of-mass mass M moving along a worldline Xµ 0 (τ) through Minkowski spacetime. The particle is prepared in a spatial superposition, with ˆ Xµ(τ) as its quantized center-of-mass po- sition. The particle has an internal oscillator degree of free- dom with position ˆq(τ), mass m, frequency ωq and couples to a scalar field ˆϕ( ˆ Xµ(τ)).

FIG. 3. (a) An inertial observer, Alice, in Minkowski vacuum, observes the decoherence of a particle undergoing uniform ac- celeration, with Bob as a co-moving observer. From Alice’s perspective the particle interacts with the vacuum field, scat- tering quantum fluctuations and experiencing a momentum recoil in the process. This recoil is associated with ‘which- path’ measurement, which leads to decoherence. (b) In Bob’s perspective, the field is thermal and the decoherence results from scattering of thermal photons.

Spin-momentum entanglement.note: The paper directly studies the decoherence of the center-of-mass motion (external) due to its interaction with an internal oscillator (internal) in a relativistic context.
